questions.question: Define normalization and explain its main objectives in database design.
keyPoints: Normalization organizes data into tables to minimize redundancy
Eliminates insertion, update, and deletion anomalies
Ensures data integrity and consistency
Achieves atomic values and proper dependencies

questions.question: Explain First Normal Form (1NF) with an example of a table that violates 1NF and how to convert it to 1NF.
keyPoints: 1NF requires atomic (indivisible) values in each column
No repeating groups or arrays allowed
Each row must be unique
Example: Phone numbers in single cell with comma-separated values violates 1NF

questions.question: What is the difference between partial dependency and transitive dependency? Give examples.
keyPoints: Partial dependency: non-key attribute depends on part of composite candidate key
Transitive dependency: non-key attribute depends on another non-key attribute
Partial dependency violates 2NF, transitive violates 3NF
Both lead to data redundancy and anomalies

questions.question: Define Boyce-Codd Normal Form (BCNF) and state how it differs from Third Normal Form (3NF).
keyPoints: BCNF: for every FD X→Y, X must be a superkey
3NF relaxes this: X can be non-superkey if Y is a prime attribute
BCNF is stricter than 3NF
BCNF may not always preserve all dependencies

questions.question: What are functional dependencies? Define terms: determinant, dependent, prime attribute, and non-prime attribute.
keyPoints: Functional dependency X→Y means X determines Y
Determinant: left side (X) of FD
Dependent: right side (Y) of FD
Prime attribute: part of any candidate key
Non-prime attribute: not part of any candidate key

questions.question: What are database anomalies? Explain insertion, deletion, and update anomalies with examples.
keyPoints: Insertion anomaly: cannot add data without other unrelated data
Deletion anomaly: deleting one record unintentionally removes other data
Update anomaly: updating one record requires updating multiple records
Caused by poor design and data redundancy

questions.question: Consider relation R(A,B,C,D) with FDs: A→BCD, B→C. Identify the highest normal form and justify your answer.
keyPoints: Candidate key is A (since A→BCD covers all attributes)
FD B→C is partial dependency (B is part of A, but not full key)
This violates 2NF
Also creates transitive dependency through B
Relation is only in 1NF

questions.question: Explain the complete process of normalizing a database from 1NF to BCNF with a suitable example. Show decomposition at each step and identify anomalies removed at each level.
keyPoints: Start with unnormalized table, convert to 1NF (atomic values)
2NF: remove partial dependencies from composite keys
3NF: remove transitive dependencies
BCNF: ensure all determinants are superkeys
Show each decomposition with schema and FDs
Explain how each level removes specific anomalies
Discuss trade-offs like lossless-join and dependency preservation

questions.question: Given relation STUDENT(StudentID, Name, Course, Instructor, Grade) with FDs: StudentID→Name; Course→Instructor; StudentID, Course→Grade. Determine: (i) Candidate keys (ii) Highest normal form (iii) Decompose into 3NF (iv) Justify each decomposition.
keyPoints: StudentID, Course together form candidate key (need both for Grade)
StudentID→Name violates 2NF (partial dependency)
Course→Instructor violates 3NF (transitive dependency)
Highest form is 1NF
Decompose: Student(StudentID, Name), Enrollment(StudentID, Course, Grade), Course(Course, Instructor)
Verify lossless-join and dependency preservation

questions.question: Define and differentiate between 1NF, 2NF, 3NF, and BCNF with respect to: (a) Conditions for satisfying each normal form (b) Types of dependencies eliminated (c) Practical examples showing violations.
keyPoints: 1NF: Atomic values, no repeating groups
2NF: No partial dependencies (for composite keys)
3NF: No transitive dependencies
BCNF: All determinants are superkeys
Provide examples for each violating condition
Explain real-world scenarios where each violation occurs

questions.question: A relation R(A,B,C,D,E) has FDs: A→B, A→C, C→D, D→E. Perform the following: (i) Find all candidate keys of R (ii) Identify all normal forms satisfied (iii) Decompose R into BCNF showing each step (iv) Check if decomposition is lossless-join and dependency preserving.
keyPoints: A+ = A,B,C,D,E = all attributes, so A is candidate key
Only A is candidate key (no other single attribute determines all)
A→B, A→C are partial (violates 2NF)
C→D, D→E are transitive (violates 3NF and BCNF)
Decompose: R1(A,B,C), R2(C,D), R3(D,E) or similar
Verify lossless-join using chase test or formula
Check if original FDs can be enforced in decomposed relations
